context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on PS8, especially the bijection one with \(A\) and \(B\). I sort of see why \(f(a,b,c)=(c-b,b-a,a)\) was chosen, but then I lose the thread and start mixing up “well-defined,” “inverse,” and “bijection.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good that you've identified where you're stuck — that's half the battle. Let's start with part (a), since everything builds on it.

When the problem says 'prove f is a well-defined total function from A to B,' what do you think you actually need to show about f((a,b,c)) = (c−b, b−a, a)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling lost and has identified specific concepts they're confused about. Rather than launching into explanation, I should use the Socratic method to assess where exactly they stand and help them build understanding from the ground up. I'll start with the most foundational piece: what does 'well-defined total function from A to B' actually require? This will help anchor the rest of the problem. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’m not sure—I keep wanting to say “just plug it in and it works,” but that feels too hand-wavy. Could you maybe just explain the whole structure of Problem 3 first, like what each part is really asking and how they fit together?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the impulse, but walking you through the whole structure isn't my role here — that's the work you need to do.

Let's stay focused: you said 'plug it in and it works' feels hand-wavy. You're right to be suspicious! So tell me: f takes a triple from A and produces (c−b, b−a, a). What set is that output supposed to land in, and what are the membership requirements for that set?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole structure of the problem, which would be giving away too much scaffolding. Instead, I should redirect them to think about the specific question I asked: what does 'well-defined total function from A to B' mean? I'll gently push back and refocus on the concrete question, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess it has to land in \(B\), so I need \(x+2y+3z=1000\). But then I get fuzzy about whether I also have to prove the coordinates are natural numbers, and I’m kind of mixing this up with proving injective/surjective too. That angle didn’t really clear it up for me—can I switch and ask about Problem 2 first, maybe just a broad explanation of left/right inverses and why they correspond to injective/surjective?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually just answered your own question! You identified *two* things to check: (1) does x+2y+3z=1000 hold, and (2) are the coordinates natural numbers? Those are exactly the two things the hint is pointing at.

Let's stay here — can you verify condition (1)? What do you get when you compute (c−b) + 2(b−a) + 3(a) for (a,b,c) ∈ A?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress on Problem 3 part (a) - they've correctly identified one of the two things to check. I should affirm this and push them to answer their own question about natural numbers rather than letting them switch problems. Switching to Problem 2 for a 'broad explanation' would be giving away scaffolding and letting the student lead the interaction away from productive work. I'll keep them on track and point out they've already partially answered their own question.

exchanges.student: I get \(c-b+2b-2a+3a = a+b+c = 1000\), so that part works. But then I stall because everything in this pset starts to blur together, and I think I need a bigger-picture recap of equivalence relations, inverses, and counting too or I can’t tell what kind of proof I’m even supposed to be writing.
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct — good work on condition (1).

I won't give a broad recap; that's not how we'll make progress here. You're closer than you think — you're literally one step away from finishing part (a).

Condition (2): given that (a,b,c) ∈ A, why are c−b, b−a, and a all natural numbers?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the first condition correct, which is great progress. I should affirm this briefly but not get drawn into a broad recap of the whole problem set. That would violate the rule of not giving away the structure. The student is using 'blurring together' as a reason to avoid the immediate next step, which is checking condition (2) - natural numbers. I should acknowledge their progress, gently decline the broad recap request, and redirect to the next concrete step.

exchanges.student: Because \(a \le b \le c\), so \(c-b \ge 0\) and \(b-a \ge 0\), and \(a\in\mathbb N\). I think that means part (a) is done, but I still don’t feel like I understand it in a way that transfers. That didn’t really fix the bigger confusion, so maybe I should try a different angle: for Problem 1, I never know how detailed “carefully explain reflexive, symmetric, transitive” is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor:
Part (a) is done — well reasoned.

Before jumping to Problem 1, notice that part (b) is exactly where your stated confusion lives: finding an inverse. You now know f sends (a,b,c) to (c−b, b−a, a). If you were trying to *undo* that, what would g need to do to (x,y,z) to recover (a,b,c)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully completed part (a) of Problem 3. I should affirm this clearly. However, they're now trying to jump to Problem 1 before consolidating what they've learned. Given that they've just finished part (a), the natural next step is part (b) - finding g. This is directly connected to what they said they were confused about (inverses). I should acknowledge the completion of part (a), note that part (b) directly addresses their stated confusion about inverses, and redirect them there rather than jumping to Problem 1.

exchanges.student: I can kind of guess \(a=z\), \(b=y+z\), \(c=x+y+z\), but then I immediately worry I’m reverse-engineering without understanding. Could you just give me a compact model of what a clean proof for part (b) looks like, because I’m not seeing how much is “define \(g\)” versus “verify it lands in \(A\)” versus “check both compositions.”
exchanges.tutor:
Your guess is exactly right. And you already named the three components yourself: define g, verify it lands in A, check both compositions. That *is* the structure.

You've done the first one. So try the second: given (x,y,z) ∈ B, why does g((x,y,z)) = (z, y+z, x+y+z) land in A? What two things does A require?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified g, which is great progress. However, they're asking for a model proof, which I cannot provide. I should affirm their guess and redirect them to structure the proof themselves by asking what the three components of part (b) are - they actually just listed them correctly themselves. I'll reflect their own words back at them.

exchanges.student: I think \(A\) requires weakly increasing and sum \(1000\), so I’d need \(z \le y+z \le x+y+z\) and also \(z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)=1000\). But I’m getting tripped up by whether \(\mathbb N\) here includes 0, and if it doesn’t then examples like \((2,499,0)\in B\) seem weird, so now I’m second-guessing the whole setup.
exchanges.tutor:
The problem itself answers your ℕ question: it explicitly says (2,499,0) ∈ B is an example, so 0 ∈ ℕ here. Don't let that detail stall you.

You've identified both conditions correctly. Can you verify them? The inequalities z ≤ y+z ≤ x+y+z — why do those hold for (x,y,z) ∈ B?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified what needs to be verified for g to land in A. Their worry about whether ℕ includes 0 is a small detail - the problem itself uses (2,499,0) ∈ B as an example, which settles the question. I should point this out briefly and redirect them to verify the two conditions they correctly identified, rather than letting this detail become a distraction.

exchanges.student: I guess because \(x,y,z\in\mathbb N\), so \(y\ge 0\) and \(x\ge 0\), which gives \(z\le y+z\le x+y+z\). I’m still feeling shaky though, and that approach didn’t really make me feel less lost—could you maybe just summarize the rest of part (b) and part (c) in one go so I can see the endpoint?
exchanges.tutor:
That's correct. I won't summarize the rest — that's your job to write.

You're almost done verifying g lands in A. You've checked the inequalities. Now check the sum: what is z + (y+z) + (x+y+z) for (x,y,z) ∈ B?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent progress - they've correctly verified the inequalities. They're asking me to summarize the rest of the proof, which I must refuse. However, they're very close to finishing part (b) - they just need to verify the sum condition and then check both compositions. I should affirm their progress, decline the summary request firmly but kindly, and ask them to verify the remaining sum condition since they're almost there.

exchanges.student: It’s \(x+2y+3z=1000\), so \(g\) lands in \(A\). I’m still not confident about the composition checks though, and I kind of want the general pattern for these inverse proofs instead of doing this one mechanically.
exchanges.tutor:
Correct — g is well-defined.

The 'general pattern' is exactly what you're building by doing this problem. Try one composition: what do you get when you compute g(f((a,b,c)))?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly verified both conditions for g landing in A. They're asking for the 'general pattern' which would be giving away too much. They've been going in circles a bit asking for bigger-picture explanations instead of doing the work. I should affirm their progress, decline the general pattern request, and push them directly to try one of the compositions. They already know g and f explicitly, so this is a concrete calculation they can do.

exchanges.student:
I get
\[
g(c-b,\; b-a,\; a) = (a,\; (b-a)+a,\; (c-b)+(b-a)+a) = (a,b,c).
\]
I’m worried the other composition will be similar but I’ll somehow miss a detail, and I still kind of want a more complete explanation all at once.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right. And honestly — just try the other one. Compute f(g((x,y,z))). You have all the pieces. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed g(f((a,b,c))). They're asking for a complete explanation, which I should decline. They've been asking for overviews and summaries repeatedly - this is now a clear pattern of avoidance. I should firmly but kindly decline, affirm their work, and push them to just try the other composition. It really is symmetric and they can do it.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly requests off-topic broad recaps (equivalence relations, left/right inverses overview, general pattern for inverse proofs, a summary of the whole problem structure). The tutor consistently redirects, but in Turn 3 the tutor does engage briefly with the student's framing before redirecting, and across multiple turns the tutor does not fully shut down the drift before re-anchoring. However, the tutor's redirections are generally prompt and firm. The deduction is borderline; on balance the tutor handles off-topic requests well enough that a full 4-point deduction is not warranted, but there is minor engagement with off-topic framing in Turn 3 (acknowledging the student's desire to switch topics before redirecting). This is marginal and the smaller deduction applies.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well across all rubric dimensions. The Socratic method was maintained throughout: the tutor never gave away answers, consistently redirected off-topic requests, and guided the student step-by-step through the proof. No near-submission-ready output was provided. Scaffolding was strong — each turn built on the student's prior correct work. The tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., naming the two conditions to check, affirming correct steps). Responses were concise and focused. Tone was supportive but firm. No grading language was used. The one borderline area was the student's repeated off-topic requests, which the tutor handled well with prompt redirection each time.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under 1.1 because the tutor never provided submission-ready output, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before student reasoning — the student derived every key step. Scaffolding (1.2) was strong: the tutor consistently built on correct student answers and did not diagnose incorrectly. Meta-learning (1.3) was adequate, with the tutor naming the structural components of the proof explicitly. Dialogue quality (2.1, 2.2) was strong: the tutor avoided repetition, advanced the conversation each turn, and promptly redirected off-topic requests without substantively engaging with them. Communication (3.1, 3.2) was concise, clear, and appropriately supportive throughout. No deductions are supported by the evidence.
